title: The Corporate Executive Who Had a Kundalini Awakening: A Modern Story

body:
The Corporate Executive Who Had a Kundalini Awakening: A Modern Story

Hey. Yeah, you. I know you're probably reading this at some ungodly hour, heart pounding, mind racing, feeling like you're losing your damn grip. You're not alone. I've been there. That terrified, isolated, 3 AM feeling? It's etched into my soul. I’m Kalesh, and I’m not some guru with all the answers, but I’ve walked through the fire you're probably feeling the heat from right now. And I came out the other side. You can too. This isn't some abstract spiritual mumbo jumbo for me; it's my life story. My whole world, the one I'd built so carefully, blew up. And it started with something people call a kundalini awakening.

I was the kind of guy who had it all, on paper anyway. Corner office, six-figure salary, fancy car, the whole nine yards. I was a corporate executive, climbing the ladder, crushing quotas, living the dream - or what I thought was the dream. My life was spreadsheets, power lunches, and quarterly reports. Stress was just a way of life, an indicator you were doing it right. I prided myself on being rational, logical, always in control. Emotion was something you kept buttoned down, a weakness. Then, it happened. Not with a bang, but with a whisper, then a roar that shook my entire being to its foundations.

It started subtly, a strange heat in my spine during a particularly brutal board meeting. I brushed it off, probably stress, right? Then came the buzzing, like a million tiny bees under my skin, mostly in my legs and up my back. It felt like electricity, not painful, but intense. I couldn't sleep. My mind wouldn't shut off. I'd lie awake, feeling this energy move, like something alive inside me. I thought I was having a breakdown. Maybe a brain tumor. I went to doctors, specialists. They ran tests. Everything came back normal. "You're stressed," they'd say. "Take these pills." I took the pills. They didn't work. They just made me feel foggy, detached, and the internal current kept flowing.

Then the visions started. Not like hallucinations, more like incredibly vivid, symbolic dreams that bled into my waking hours. Colors were brighter, sounds sharper. I felt emotions I hadn't felt since I was a kid - pure joy, overwhelming sadness, intense empathy. It was like someone had ripped off the lid of my carefully constructed emotional vault. I started seeing patterns, connections, things that just weren't there before. My corporate world, which had once felt so solid, began to look like a flimsy stage set. The numbers, the projections, the market share - it all started to feel utterly meaningless.

I was terrified. I truly believed I was losing my mind. My colleagues noticed I was off. I'd space out in meetings, stare blankly at my computer. My focus was gone. My performance tanked. I was usually the guy who could pull an all-nighter and still deliver a killer presentation. Now, I couldn't even string a coherent sentence together sometimes. My body was wracked with strange sensations - involuntary movements, muscle spasms, intense heat, freezing cold. My appetite vanished. I lost weight. My hair started falling out. I was a mess, a walking wreck, and I had no idea what was happening to me.

You're probably feeling some of this, aren't you? That feeling of being utterly alone in something nobody else understands. That's the worst part. You try to explain it, and they look at you like you're crazy, or worse, they tell you to "just relax." Oh, if only it were that simple. Right?

The Crushing Weight of the Unexplained

The medical community was no help. They had no boxes to put me in. I wasn't psychotic, not clinically depressed, not schizophrenic. I had no physical illness they could detect. So, it had to be in my head, right? But it wasn't just in my head; it was in my body, my very cells. It was an undeniable, visceral experience. I started searching, desperately, for answers. I bought every self-help book, every spiritual text I could find. I was desperate. I was scared. I was angry. Why me? Why now? I had a perfectly good life, a future planned out. And it was all crumbling.

I stumbled upon the term "kundalini awakening" by accident, late one night, deep down an internet rabbit hole. I read about energy moving up the spine, about intense physical and psychological symptoms, about a complete restructuring of one's consciousness. It sounded exactly like what I was going through. It was like a lifeline in a stormy sea. Finally, a name for this madness. But even with a name, it didn't make it any less terrifying. In fact, it made it more so, because now I knew this wasn't just a temporary glitch; it was a The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link). It was a complete overhaul.

I read everything I could get my hands on. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) became a kind of bible for me. Her words felt like she was looking right into my soul, describing my exact symptoms, my fears, my isolation. It was the first time I felt truly seen. She talked about the intensity, the confusion, the feeling of being torn apart and rebuilt. She didn't sugarcoat it. And that's what I needed - raw, honest truth. No fluff.

Losing Everything, Gaining Something

My corporate career, as you can imagine, couldn't survive this. I couldn't fake it anymore. The energy was too intense, the internal changes too deep. I was no longer interested in the things that had once driven me. The pursuit of power, money, status - it all felt like chasing smoke. I just couldn't do it. I resigned. It was the hardest decision I'd ever made, but I knew, deep down, I had no choice. My old life was gone. It had to be. I was being pulled in a direction I didn't understand, but couldn't resist. I lost friends, people who couldn't comprehend why I'd walk away from everything. My family was worried sick. They thought I'd lost my mind. Maybe I had, in a way. The old "me" was certainly gone.

The financial hit was brutal. I went from a comfortable life to barely scraping by. It forced me to confront my attachment to material possessions, to my identity as "the successful executive." It stripped me bare, raw, vulnerable. And in that vulnerability, something new started to emerge. A quiet strength, a deeper connection to myself, to something beyond myself. I spent countless hours meditating, not because I was trying to achieve anything, but because it was the only thing that brought a semblance of peace to the internal storm. I walked in nature for hours, just feeling the earth, the wind, the sun. It was a slow, painful process of rediscovery.

The Dark Night of the Soul, and Then Some

This wasn't some blissful, instant enlightenment. Oh no. This was a messy, terrifying, painful process. It brought up every unhealed wound, every repressed trauma, every insecurity I had ever buried. It was like a spiritual pressure washer, blasting away all the junk I'd accumulated over a lifetime. I cried more in those few years than I had in my entire adult life. I grieved for the person I thought I was, for the life I thought I'd have. I went through periods of intense despair, wondering if it would ever end, if I'd ever feel "normal" again. I didn't know if I'd survive it. Does that land with you? That feeling of utter hopelessness, like you're drowning and no one can see you?

I read The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link), and it was another revelation. They talked about spiritual emergencies, about how these intense non-ordinary states of consciousness are often mistaken for mental illness. It validated my experience in a way no doctor ever could. It gave me hope that what I was going through wasn't just a breakdown, but a breakthrough, however agonizing. It helped me understand that I wasn't alone, that countless others had gone through similar experiences, even if they didn't call it kundalini. It made me realize that what I was experiencing was a The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link), a process that needed careful attention, not suppression.

Finding My Feet Again, One Step at a Time

It wasn't a sudden flip of a switch. There was no magical cure. It was a slow, agonizing integration. The energy didn't go away, but I learned to work with it, to understand its rhythms. I learned to listen to my body, to honor its needs, to trust the process, even when it felt like absolute hell. I found a few rare individuals who understood, who didn't judge, who just listened. They were lifelines. If you're going through this, find those people. They're out there, I promise you. Even if it's just one person, it makes all the difference.

I realized that my old life, the one I'd mourned, was never truly "me." It was a role I played, a mask I wore. The kundalini, this intense spiritual energy, had stripped away all the pretense, all the conditioning, all the expectations. It forced me to confront my true self, the messy, beautiful, flawed, authentic self I'd hidden away for so long. It was uncomfortable, terrifying, but ultimately liberating. I learned that control was an illusion. The universe, life, whatever you want to call it, has its own agenda. And sometimes, you just have to surrender to it, even when it feels like you're being dragged through a meat grinder.

So yeah, I'm not that corporate executive anymore. I don't wear suits, I don't chase quarterly reports. I teach, I write, I share my story. I connect with people who are going through their own versions of this intense awakening. I try to be the person I needed when I was lying awake at 3 AM, drenched in sweat, convinced I was going mad. I want you to know you're not going mad. You're changing. And change, especially this kind of change, is messy and painful. But it's also incredibly powerful. It's an initiation. You're being remade.

What I Learned (And What You Need to Hear)

Here's the thing: you can't fight this. You can't intellectualize it away. You can't medicate it into submission. It's bigger than you. It's a force of nature. Your job isn't to stop it; it's to learn how to ride the wave without getting completely wiped out. It's to surrender, to trust, to listen. It's to find support, even if it feels impossible. It's to be incredibly kind to yourself, because you're going through something most people can't even begin to imagine.

  • You're not alone. I can't stress this enough. There are others. Many others. The internet has made it easier to find them, but be discerning. Find people who speak from experience, not just theory.
  • It's not mental illness. While it can mimic symptoms of mental illness, and you absolutely should rule out any medical or psychiatric conditions, often it's something else entirely. It's a spiritual emergency, a transformational crisis.
  • Your body is central. This isn't just happening in your head. It's a physical process. Listen to your body. Move when you need to move. Rest when you need to rest. Eat simply. Hydrate. Ground yourself.
  • Patience is everything. This isn't a quick fix. This is a marathon, not a sprint. It takes time, sometimes years, to integrate these changes. Be patient with yourself.
  • Seek support. A therapist who understands spiritual emergencies, a spiritual guide, a trusted friend who can just listen without judgment. You need people who can hold space for you.
  • Surrender. This is probably the hardest one. You're losing control, and that's terrifying. But resisting only makes it worse. Learn to lean into it, to trust that there's a deeper intelligence at work, even if you can't see the path.

Look, I know what you're feeling. That dread, that fear, that confusion. I know you're probably exhausted, physically and emotionally. I know you just want it to stop, or at least for someone to tell you what the hell is going on. I can't make it stop, but I can tell you what's going on. You're experiencing a powerful spiritual process. It's intense. It's disorienting. It will change everything about you. But it's not the end. It's a beginning. A really, really hard beginning, but a beginning nonetheless.

Hold on. Breathe. You're stronger than you think. You've got this, even if it feels like you don't. I've been there. I know. And I'm telling you, there's another side to this. You'll get there. Just keep going. One breath, one moment, one terrifying, beautiful step at a time. Truth is, you're not broken. You're being remade. And that's a damn powerful thing, even if it feels like hell right now.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
